ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No dedicated FoodEx2 marshmallow code confirmed; A003K (sugar confectionery) used as closest parent category anchor. Reviewer should verify against current FoodEx2 hierarchy.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT food concept confirmed for marshmallow confection; left null to preserve ontology honesty.

allergens: gelatin (pork or beef derived — not suitable for vegetarians, vegans, or certain religious dietary requirements)

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical single serving of marshmallows is approximately 28 g (about 4 regular-sized pieces), providing roughly 89 kcal and 23 g of sugar.

nutrient_source.title: Marshmallows — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 19116)
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture (USDA) Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA SR Legacy entry 19116 'Candies, marshmallows' used as primary nutrient anchor. Singapore HPB database values for energy (319 kcal), carbohydrate (82.1 g), sugar (82.1 g), sodium (39 mg), dietary fibre (0.0 g), calcium (18 mg), and iron (1.4 mg) are consistent with USDA data and are retained. Protein and fat are reported as -1 in the HPB source; USDA SR Legacy reports protein 1.8 g and fat 0.2 g per 100 g for standard marshmallows. These USDA values are adopted for protein_g and fat_g to resolve the -1 sentinel. See notes for full explanation.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Foster-Powell K, Holt SHA, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: Marshmallow GI is estimated at approximately 62 (medium) based on the University of Sydney GI Database entry for marshmallows and corroborated by Foster-Powell et al. (2008) international GI tables which list marshmallow at GI ~62 (glucose reference = 100). Glycemic load per 28 g serving = (62 × 23.0 g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 14.3 (medium GL). Available carbohydrate per serving = 82.1 g × 0.28 = 23.0 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Extreme sugar load (82.1 g/100 g) with zero dietary fibre drives insulin-mediated sodium retention and promotes endothelial dysfunction, outweighing the relatively low direct sodium content of 39 mg/100 g.
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should avoid marshmallows entirely or restrict intake to an occasional 1–2 piece portion, as the 82.1 g/100 g sugar content promotes insulin-mediated sodium retention and vascular inflammation despite the low direct sodium level.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar content of 82.1 g/100 g with zero dietary fibre and a medium-high GI (~62) produces rapid, pronounced postprandial blood glucose elevation; even a 28 g serving delivers 23 g of free sugars.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid marshmallows entirely, as the 82.1 g/100 g free-sugar content with zero fibre and a GI of approximately 62 causes immediate and clinically significant blood glucose spikes that are incompatible with glycaemic management.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Ultra-processed, high-free-sugar confection (82.1 g sugar/100 g, zero fibre, negligible micronutrients) with no cardioprotective nutrients; habitual consumption is associated with elevated triglycerides, weight gain, and increased cardiovascular risk.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should avoid marshmallows, as this ultra-processed confection provides 82.1 g of free sugar per 100 g with no fibre, no cardioprotective fats, and no beneficial micronutrients, contributing to hypertriglyceridaemia and excess caloric intake.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Pure refined-sugar confection (82.1 g sugar/100 g, zero fibre) with no anti-inflammatory nutrients promotes pro-inflammatory mediator production and is contraindicated in inflammatory joint disease management.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid marshmallows entirely, as the concentrated refined sugar content (82.1 g/100 g) with zero fibre and no anti-inflammatory factors actively promotes systemic inflammation.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Marshmallow is a soft, light, and highly aerated confection with origins in 19th-century Europe and now most closely associated with American confectionery culture. It is manufactured by dissolving sugar and glucose syrup in water, blooming gelatin separately, then combining the two and whipping the mixture at high speed to incorporate large volumes of air, producing a foam that is stabilised by the gelatin network as it cools. The resulting mass is extruded or deposited into moulds, allowed to set, and cut into pillow-shaped pieces that are dusted with a mixture of cornstarch and icing sugar to prevent sticking. The texture is characteristically spongy, springy, and yielding, with a melt-in-the-mouth quality driven by the high air content and the thermoreversible gelatin matrix. The flavour is intensely sweet with a mild vanilla note, and the exterior has a slightly dry, powdery surface that contrasts with the soft interior. Marshmallows are consumed as a standalone sweet snack, floated on hot chocolate or cocoa, toasted over open flames or a gas burner until the exterior caramelises and the interior liquefies (a preparation central to the North American campfire tradition of s'mores), and incorporated as a functional ingredient in baked goods and confectionery such as rice crispy treats, fudge, rocky road, and sweet potato casserole. Nutritionally, marshmallows are almost entirely free sugar: 82.1 g of sugar per 100 g, with negligible protein (1.8 g), minimal fat (0.2 g), and zero dietary fibre, delivering 319 kcal per 100 g. The glycemic index is approximately 62 (medium GI), but the extreme sugar density means even a small 28 g serving (approximately 4 regular pieces) delivers 23 g of free sugars and a glycemic load of approximately 14, which is clinically significant. Clinically, marshmallows are contraindicated for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes due to the 82.1 g/100 g free-sugar load with zero fibre producing immediate and pronounced postprandial glucose spikes. For hypertension, the extreme sugar content drives insulin-mediated sodium retention and endothelial dysfunction despite the relatively low direct sodium of 39 mg/100 g. For cardiovascular disease, habitual consumption of this ultra-processed, high-free-sugar confection is associated with elevated triglycerides and excess caloric intake with no cardioprotective benefit. For rheumatoid arthritis, the concentrated refined sugar actively promotes pro-inflammatory mediator production with no offsetting anti-inflammatory nutrients. Marshmallows contain animal-derived gelatin (typically porcine or bovine collagen) and are therefore not suitable for vegetarians, vegans, or individuals following halal or kosher dietary requirements unless the product is specifically certified. Standard marshmallows are naturally gluten-free and dairy-free. Calcium (18 mg/100 g) and iron (1.4 mg/100 g) are present in small amounts, likely from fortification or incidental mineral content, but are nutritionally insignificant in the context of typical serving sizes.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a marshmallow?
What is the sugar content of marshmallows?
Can I eat marshmallows if I have high blood pressure?
What is the glycemic index of marshmallows?
How much sugar is in a marshmallow?

confidence_reason: Primary nutrient values for energy, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre, calcium, and iron are sourced directly from the Singapore HPB database (confidence 0.95). Protein and fat values are resolved from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 19116), an approved primary source with an exact match for standard marshmallows. GI value of approximately 62 is corroborated by the University of Sydney GI Database and Foster-Powell et al. (2008) international GI tables. All sources are approved under Project Veritas v2 source policy.
notes: 1. NEGATIVE SENTINEL RESOLUTION: The input HPB record reported protein = -1.0 and fat = -1.0 (data not available in HPB source). These have been resolved using USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy FDC ID 19116 'Candies, marshmallows', which reports protein_g = 1.8 g/100 g and fat_g = 0.2 g/100 g. The -1 sentinels have been replaced with these USDA values. 2. SATURATED FAT: USDA SR Legacy reports saturated fat as 0.0 g/100 g for standard marshmallows; this is a confirmed zero by formulation — marshmallows contain negligible fat and no significant saturated fatty acid fraction. Confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy FDC ID 19116. 3. CHOLESTEROL: Reported as 0.0 mg/100 g per USDA SR Legacy; confirmed zero — gelatin-based confection with no cholesterol-bearing lipid fraction. Confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy FDC ID 19116. 4. MONOUNSATURATED FAT, POLYUNSATURATED FAT, TRANS FAT: Set to -1.0; detailed fatty acid breakdown not reported in HPB or USDA SR Legacy for this product category. 5. MICRONUTRIENTS (vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg): Set to -1.0; these micronutrients are not reported in the HPB database entry or USDA SR Legacy for marshmallows, consistent with the negligible micronutrient profile of this ultra-processed sugar confection. 6. POTASSIUM: 5.0 mg/100 g per USDA SR Legacy; retained as a positive numeric value. 7. WATER: 16.4 g/100 g per USDA SR Legacy. 8. DIETARY FLAGS: Halal and kosher status set to false as standard marshmallows use conventional animal gelatin; halal-certified or kosher-certified variants exist commercially but are not the generic product. 9. ONTOLOGY: FoodEx2 code A003K used as closest_parent category anchor for sugar confectionery; reviewer should verify against current FoodEx2 release. FoodOn URI FOODON_03301710 used as closest defensible match; reviewer should confirm against current FoodOn release. SNOMED CT left null — no confirmed SNOMED food concept for marshmallow confection identified. 10. MULTILINGUAL NAMES: Marshmallow is a Western/international confection with no established local-language names in Singaporean, Japanese, Korean, Indonesian, Thai, or Vietnamese culinary traditions; local_names fields are set to null. 11. ZERO MACRO JUSTIFICATION: saturated_fat_g is confirmed zero by product formulation — marshmallows contain negligible total fat with no significant saturated fatty acid fraction, confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy FDC ID 19116. dietary_fibre_g is confirmed zero per both Singapore HPB database and USDA SR Legacy; marshmallows are a refined sugar confection containing no fibre-bearing ingredients.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 1: Initial draft constructed from input JSON; protein and fat resolved from USDA SR Legacy FDC 19116 to replace HPB -1 sentinels; GI sourced from University of Sydney GI Database / Foster-Powell et al. (2008); health context expanded to include cardiovascular disease; ontology anchors added.
Iteration 2: Corrected ontology.foodex2_match_type from 'analogous_estimate' to 'closest_parent'; verified natural_language_description length and content.

Iteration 3: Substantially expanded natural_language_description to explicitly cover manufacturing process (dissolving, gelatin blooming, high-speed whipping, foam stabilisation, extrusion/moulding, cornstarch dusting), detailed sensory profile (spongy/springy texture, melt-in-mouth quality, air content, gelatin matrix, powdery exterior vs soft interior contrast), cultural and historical context (19th-century European origins, American confectionery association, campfire s'mores tradition), full range of culinary applications, quantified clinical guidance for all four health conditions, and dietary suitability detail. This resolves the q6_llm_ready_semantics failure from iteration 2 validator feedback.
